_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: Everyone assumes they already know her. That's exactly how she likes it.

museum_objects.title: Dominion of Canada $1 Note (1870)
museum_objects.description: An early Dominion of Canada dollar note, issued just three years after Confederation.
museum_objects.significance: Everyone assumed this note was just a copy of American or British money — it wasn't. Isabella was quietly building her own identity from her very first printing.
museum_objects.plain_language: One of the very first Canadian dollar bills, printed just a few years after Canada became its own country.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so. | IMAGE QA FAIL: the image is a painting, not a banknote

museum_objects.title: Canada Victoria 20 Cents Coin (1871)
museum_objects.description: An early Canadian silver coin bearing the portrait of Queen Victoria.
museum_objects.significance: A coin with a British queen's face, but Canadian words and Canadian purpose — Isabella's split identity started on day one.
museum_objects.plain_language: An old Canadian coin from 1871 with Queen Victoria's face on it, from Canada's earliest years as a country.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so. | IMAGE QA FAIL: The image is a painting, not a coin, and does not depict Queen Victoria or any coin from Canada.

museum_objects.title: British Columbia $20 Gold Pattern Coin (1862)
museum_objects.description: A rare pattern coin proposed for the colony of British Columbia before it joined Confederation.
museum_objects.significance: Before Canada existed as we know it, this gold coin imagined a completely different currency future — a road Isabella never took, but almost did.
museum_objects.plain_language: A rare gold coin design proposed for British Columbia before it even joined Canada — a currency path that never happened.

museum_objects.title: Hudson's Bay Company Made Beaver Token
museum_objects.description: A trade token used by the Hudson's Bay Company, valued in units equivalent to one prime beaver pelt.
museum_objects.significance: Long before Isabella had a face or a number, Canada's economy ran on beaver pelts — this little brass token is the ancestor of everything she'd become.
museum_objects.plain_language: An old trade token used by fur traders in Canada, worth one beaver pelt — Canada's economy before it had its own dollar.

museum_objects.title: A History of the Canadian Dollar (Bank of Canada)
museum_objects.description: The Bank of Canada's own comprehensive published history of the Canadian dollar.
museum_objects.significance: This book is Isabella's own autobiography, written by the institution that has managed her identity since Confederation.
museum_objects.plain_language: The Bank of Canada's own official book telling the complete history of the Canadian dollar.

world_map.map_description: Isabella's dollar emerged from Confederation-era Canada, shaped by fur trade economics, gold rushes, and a careful balance between British and American monetary influence.

nodes.label: Ottawa (Parliament)
nodes.description: Canada's capital, where post-Confederation monetary decisions were made and the Bank of Canada now operates.

nodes.label: Cariboo Gold Rush Region, BC
nodes.description: Site of a major 1860s gold rush that shaped British Columbia's early currency proposals before joining Confederation.

nodes.label: Montreal (McCord Museum)
nodes.description: Historic financial center of early Canada, home to a museum documenting the country's monetary artifacts.

nodes.label: Nova Scotia
nodes.description: One of the founding provinces of Confederation, with its own pre-Confederation currency history.

nodes.label: Newfoundland
nodes.description: Held its own separate currency until joining Canada in 1949, illustrating the gradual unification of Canadian money.

historical_map_source.title: A History of the Canadian Dollar
